title: Lab Grown vs. Natural Diamonds: What's the Difference?
content:
Few decisions in the engagement-ring journey spark as much curiosity as the choice between a lab grown diamond and a natural one. The good news: you are not choosing between "real" and "fake." Both are genuine diamonds. At Boldradiant we believe the more you understand the difference, the more confident your choice will be — so here is a clear, side-by-side look at what unites these two stones and what sets them apart.

What is a lab grown diamond?

A lab grown diamond is a real diamond created in a controlled laboratory rather than deep within the earth. It begins with a tiny carbon seed and is grown using one of two technologies — High Pressure High Temperature (HPHT) or Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD). Over a period of days to weeks, pure carbon atoms arrange themselves into the same crystal lattice found in mined diamonds. The rough stone is then cut, polished, graded and set exactly like any other diamond. In other words, the composition, structure and properties are identical to a natural diamond; the only meaningful difference is how and where it was made.

Are lab grown diamonds real diamonds?

Yes. A lab grown diamond shares the same chemical, physical and optical characteristics as a mined diamond. Both are pure crystallised carbon, both rank a perfect 10 on the Mohs hardness scale, and both display the same brilliance and fire. A lab grown diamond is not an imitation or a simulant like cubic zirconia — it is a diamond in every scientific sense. The distinction is simply one of origin: a natural diamond forms over roughly a billion years beneath the earth, while a lab grown diamond forms in weeks under carefully engineered conditions.

Lab grown vs. natural diamond: side by side

CharacteristicLab Grown DiamondNatural Diamond
CompositionPure crystallised carbonPure crystallised carbon
Hardness10 on the Mohs scale10 on the Mohs scale
BrillianceIdentical refractive indexIdentical refractive index
GradingCut, colour, clarity, caratCut, colour, clarity, carat
CertificationGraded by independent labsGraded by independent labs
OriginGrown in weeks in a labFormed over ~1 billion years
RarityCan be produced to demandFinite, geologically limited
PriceTypically more accessibleCommands a scarcity premium

What is the same?

Everything that affects how a diamond looks, performs and lasts is identical. Both stones are carbon in the same lattice, so there is no chemical difference between them. Both achieve the maximum hardness rating, making them equally suited to everyday wear. Both interact with light to produce the same sparkle, fire and scintillation, and both are evaluated on the same four Cs along the same D-to-Z colour scale. When graded by a reputable laboratory, both receive the same kind of report documenting their quality.

What is different?

The differences come down to origin, rarity and cost. Natural diamonds are finite and formed by extraordinary geological forces, which gives them a scarcity that many people find romantic. Lab grown diamonds can be produced in growing quantities as the technology matures, which means they typically cost significantly less than a comparable mined stone. That price advantage is purely a matter of supply — not quality — so the same budget often buys a larger carat weight, a higher clarity grade, or a more elaborate design.

Can you tell the difference?

To the naked eye, no. At equivalent grades, a lab grown and a natural diamond are visually indistinguishable, and even experienced jewellers cannot reliably tell them apart without specialised equipment. Under magnification a gemologist may spot subtle differences in growth patterns or inclusion types, and most lab grown stones carry a tiny laser inscription on the girdle noting their origin. Because they share the same thermal properties, lab grown diamonds also register as genuine on standard diamond testers.

How are lab grown diamonds graded and certified?

Lab grown diamonds go through exactly the same grading process as mined diamonds at respected institutions such as the GIA and IGI. Gemologists assess cut, colour, clarity and carat using identical scales and the same scrutiny. The resulting report documents the stone's quality and clearly states that it was laboratory grown, so there is full transparency about what you are buying. Every Boldradiant centre stone is independently certified, regardless of origin.

Common misconceptions

  • "They're fake." Untrue — they are chemically, physically and optically identical to natural diamonds.
  • "They look different." Untrue — experts cannot distinguish them without lab equipment.
  • "They aren't durable." Untrue — they share the same perfect 10 hardness rating.
  • "They're flawless." Untrue — like natural diamonds, they can contain inclusions and span the full clarity range.

Pros and cons of lab grown diamonds

Advantages

  • Identical beauty and durability to a mined diamond
  • More accessible pricing, so your budget stretches further
  • A smaller environmental footprint than mining
  • Available in rare fancy colours such as pink, blue and yellow

Considerations

  • They do not carry the billion-year geological rarity of a natural stone
  • Some buyers place personal value on a natural origin story

Which is right for you?

Choose a lab grown diamond if maximising value matters to you — a larger or higher-quality stone within budget — or if a lower environmental impact is a priority. Choose a natural diamond if geological rarity and a billion-year origin story carry emotional or symbolic weight for you. There is no wrong answer. The right choice is simply the one that best reflects your priorities, your values and your budget.

Frequently asked questions

What else are lab grown diamonds called?

They go by many names — lab created, lab made, man-made, engineered or cultured diamonds. All describe the same thing: a real diamond grown in a laboratory.

Do lab grown diamonds last?

Yes. They are just as durable as natural diamonds and, with proper care, will last a lifetime.

Can you insure a lab grown diamond?

Yes. Most jewellery insurers cover lab grown diamonds; you'll typically need a grading report or appraisal.

Do lab grown diamonds scratch?

They are extremely scratch-resistant. At 10 on the Mohs scale, only another diamond can scratch a diamond.
